item.title: Working with Weak Instruments
item.description:
12 min read

Background

Somewhere along the way, the econometrics profession agreed on a password. If your first-stage -statistic clears , your instrument is “strong,” and you may proceed to report your two-stage least squares (2SLS) estimate with the usual and a clear conscience. I have repeated this rule in seminars, written it into referee reports, and screened my own specifications by it. It is wrong — or at least far weaker than its ubiquity suggests — and the interesting part is that the people who have shown it to be wrong do not agree on what to do instead.

This post picks up where my earlier note on instrumental variables in randomized experiments left off. There I treated the identification side of IV — the local average treatment effect, the Wald ratio, the four assumptions that make it all work. Here I take identification for granted and worry about inference: once you have a just-identified IV estimate in hand, how do you build an honest confidence interval when the instrument is only weakly correlated with the endogenous regressor? The literature has converged on a verdict — the rule is broken — and then split into two camps over the remedy. One camp says abandon the -test and invert the Anderson–Rubin statistic instead. The other says keep the -test but stop pretending the critical value is . I will lay out both, with the mathematics, and let you see why reasonable people land in different places.

Notation

I stay in the simplest possible setting throughout: one outcome, one endogenous regressor, one instrument — the just-identified case. Index observations by and write the structural equation, first stage, and reduced form as

where is the instrument, the endogenous regressor, and correlated with — the endogeneity that motivates IV in the first place. (Any exogenous controls are partialled out of all three equations; nothing below changes.) The exclusion restriction ties the three equations together through the single restriction

The IV estimator is the ratio of the reduced-form to first-stage slopes,

the Wald estimator. The trouble lives entirely in that denominator. When is small relative to the sampling noise in , we are dividing by something close to zero, and — a ratio of two correlated normals — is badly non-normal. Its -statistic does not behave like a standard normal, no matter how large the sample. Weak means exactly this: is small relative to . The standard gauge of instrument strength is the first-stage -statistic, which in this single-instrument world is just the squared -statistic on ,

A Closer Look

Why the “F > 10” rule broke

The rule of thumb traces to Staiger and Stock (1997), who suggested as a rough indicator that weak-instrument distortions were tolerable. Stock and Yogo (2005) made it rigorous by defining “weak” through worst-case performance and tabulating critical values under two distinct criteria. The bias criterion asks how large the worst-case 2SLS bias is relative to OLS; its critical values sit near across a range of instrument counts, which is where the folklore comes from. The size criterion asks how badly a nominal 5% -test can over-reject; its critical values are a different animal, rising from about with one instrument to nearly with thirty. Already the single number “10” is doing two incompatible jobs.

The deeper problem is that the entire Stock–Yogo apparatus was derived under homoskedasticity. The critical values depend on a Kronecker-product structure in the covariance matrix that simply does not hold once errors are heteroskedastic, clustered, or serially correlated — which is to say, in essentially every applied setting. Comparing a conventional first-stage to a Stock–Yogo cutoff in a clustered design is comparing against the wrong distribution.

The effective F-statistic

The fix for measuring strength is due to Montiel Olea and Pflueger (2013), who introduced what they call the effective -statistic. The conventional first-stage and even the naive robust Wald have, as Andrews, Stock, and Sun (2019) put it, “no theoretical justification” for gauging instrument strength under heteroskedasticity — they target the wrong population object or misstate its variance. The effective replaces the variance term with a heteroskedasticity-robust analogue:

where is the instrument second-moment matrix and the robust variance of . It collapses to the usual under homoskedasticity and, crucially, “measures the right object and gets the standard errors right on average.” In the just-identified case it coincides with the robust first-stage . The practical instruction is simple: whenever you report a first-stage as a strength diagnostic, it should be the effective one, compared against the appropriate Montiel Olea–Pflueger critical values rather than a remembered “10.”

The Anderson–Rubin test

Measuring strength is only half the battle; the harder question is what to report for when strength is in doubt. The oldest and most robust answer is the Anderson–Rubin (AR) test, dating to 1949. Instead of forming , AR inverts a test. To test , construct the residual and ask whether it is correlated with the instrument. Under the null,

and the coefficient on is , which equals zero precisely when is the true (since ). So the AR statistic is just the squared -statistic for in the regression of on , and under it is distributed regardless of the value of — even at . That is the whole trick: testing whether a coefficient is zero in a clean regression does not care how strong the instrument is.

The confidence set is the collection of nulls the test fails to reject,

Because is quadratic in , the set can be a finite interval, the whole real line, or the complement of an interval. When the instrument is weak enough that a first-stage test cannot reject , the set is unbounded — and this is a feature, not a bug. Gleser and Hwang (1987) and Dufour (1997) proved that any confidence set with correct coverage for must be unbounded with positive probability. No procedure built on a finite standard error can have honest coverage under arbitrarily weak instruments. AR’s occasional infinite intervals are it telling you the truth: the data cannot pin down. In the just-identified case AR is also uniformly most powerful unbiased (Moreira, 2009) and fully efficient under strong identification, so reporting it costs nothing even when the instrument turns out to be strong.

The mainstream verdict: abandon the t-test

The Annual Review tradition — Andrews, Stock, and Sun (2019) as the canonical statement — draws the natural conclusion: in the just-identified case, report AR intervals, full stop, whatever the first-stage . Keane and Neal (2024) push this to its sharp edge and argue for abandoning the 2SLS -test even when instruments are strong. Their argument is a power asymmetry that the older literature, fixated on bias and size, had missed.

The mechanism is the 2SLS standard error itself. The estimated structural variance entering is a quadratic function of that is minimized at the OLS estimate. So whenever a draw of lands near OLS, its standard error is artificially small, and the -test is correspondingly eager to declare significance. The upshot is a test with inflated power to detect false positives in the direction of the OLS bias and almost no power to detect true effects lying away from OLS. Keane and Neal show this distortion persists at first-stage values of , , even — far above any conventional threshold — and conclude that the bar for the should be raised to roughly , and that one should simply use AR throughout. The AR standard error, by contrast, is built around a variance minimized at rather than at OLS, which is exactly what kills the asymmetry.

The counterpoint: fix the t-test instead

Lee, McCrary, Moreira, and Porter (2022) accept the diagnosis and reject the prescription. Their objection is practical: practitioners know and trust the -ratio, the entire reporting apparatus is built around it, and throwing it out is a heavy ask. The -statistic is not normal under weak instruments — true — but its non-normal distribution is known, and depends on the data only through the observed first-stage . So rather than discard it, replace the constant with a critical value that is a smooth, decreasing function of the first stage. They call this the tF procedure.

The headline number is sobering. For the constant to deliver a genuine 5% test, you do not need , or even Stock and Yogo’s — you need

Below that, is too small and you must inflate. Operationally the procedure is a lookup: estimate your usual (robust, clustered) 2SLS standard error, read the adjustment factor off their Table 3 at your observed , and multiply. A few anchor points at the 5% level make the magnitude vivid:

First-stage tF critical value SE multiplier

At the celebrated , in other words, an honest 95% interval is about wider than the one most papers report. Lee and coauthors then prove something genuinely surprising. Given AR’s optimality properties, one would expect AR intervals to dominate. Yet conditional on both procedures returning a bounded interval, the AR interval has infinite expected length while the tF interval’s is finite — so whenever both are bounded, tF is weakly shorter. The resolution is the Gleser–Hwang–Dufour theorem from before: the unavoidable infinite expected length has to live somewhere, and tF banishes all of it into the single event , where it reports the whole real line and declares defeat.

A weak-instrument example in code

The simulation below makes the stakes concrete. I generate data with a true effect of exactly zero, strong endogeneity, and a deliberately weak instrument (population first-stage around ). I first measure how often the naive 5% -test rejects the true null , then compare three confidence intervals on a single dataset: the naive , the tF-adjusted interval, and the inverted AR interval.

  • R
  • Python
set.seed(1988)

# tF SE-multipliers: Lee, McCrary, Moreira & Porter (2022), Table 3 Panel A (5% level)
tF_F    <- c(4.000, 5.002, 6.056, 7.085, 8.473, 9.835, 10.253, 13.048,
             16.618, 20.721, 24.605, 33.457, 49.495, 68.930, 104.67)
tF_mult <- c(9.519, 3.497, 2.602, 2.218, 1.935, 1.767, 1.727, 1.542,
             1.407, 1.315, 1.256, 1.173, 1.096, 1.047, 1.000)
tf_mult <- function(F) {
  if (F < 3.84)    return(Inf)   # no bounded interval below 3.84
  if (F >= 104.67) return(1.0)
  approx(tF_F, tF_mult, xout = F)$y
}

make_data <- function(n, pi, rho, beta = 0) {
  Z <- rnorm(n); v <- rnorm(n); e <- rnorm(n)
  u <- rho * v + sqrt(1 - rho^2) * e   # corr(u, v) = rho -> endogeneity
  list(Z = Z, X = pi * Z + v, Y = beta * (pi * Z + v) + u)
}

iv_fit <- function(Z, X, Y) {                 # just-identified 2SLS / Wald
  Zc <- Z - mean(Z); Xc <- X - mean(X); Yc <- Y - mean(Y)
  b  <- sum(Zc * Yc) / sum(Zc * Xc)
  r  <- Yc - b * Xc; n <- length(Z)
  se <- sqrt(sum(r^2) / (n - 1) * sum(Zc^2)) / abs(sum(Zc * Xc))
  pi_hat <- sum(Zc * Xc) / sum(Zc^2); fr <- Xc - pi_hat * Zc
  Fst <- (pi_hat / sqrt(sum(fr^2) / (n - 2) / sum(Zc^2)))^2
  c(b = b, se = se, F = Fst)
}

ar_ci <- function(Z, X, Y, grid) {            # invert the AR test, chi^2_1 cutoff
  Zc <- Z - mean(Z); n <- length(Z); keep <- logical(length(grid))
  for (i in seq_along(grid)) {
    Wc <- Y - grid[i] * X; Wc <- Wc - mean(Wc)
    s  <- sum(Zc * Wc) / sum(Zc^2); res <- Wc - s * Zc
    keep[i] <- (s / sqrt(sum(res^2) / (n - 2) / sum(Zc^2)))^2 <= 3.841
  }
  range(grid[keep])
}

n <- 200; pi <- 0.14; rho <- 0.95; reps <- 20000

# Part A: size of the naive 2SLS t-test when the null beta = 0 is TRUE
Fs <- numeric(reps); rej <- 0
for (r in seq_len(reps)) {
  d <- make_data(n, pi, rho, 0); fit <- iv_fit(d$Z, d$X, d$Y)
  Fs[r] <- fit["F"]; if (abs(fit["b"] / fit["se"]) > 1.96) rej <- rej + 1
}
cat(sprintf("mean F=%.1f  naive-t rejection @ nominal 5%% = %.3f\n",
            mean(Fs), rej / reps))

# Part B: three intervals on one weak-instrument dataset
set.seed(1988)
d <- make_data(n, pi, rho, 0); fit <- iv_fit(d$Z, d$X, d$Y)
b <- fit["b"]; se <- fit["se"]; F <- fit["F"]; m <- tf_mult(F)
ar <- ar_ci(d$Z, d$X, d$Y, seq(-5, 5, length.out = 100001))
cat(sprintf("beta_hat=%.3f  SE=%.3f  first-stage F=%.2f\n", b, se, F))
cat(sprintf("naive 95%% CI [%.3f, %.3f]\n", b - 1.96*se, b + 1.96*se))
cat(sprintf("tF    95%% CI [%.3f, %.3f]  (multiplier %.3f)\n",
            b - 1.96*m*se, b + 1.96*m*se, m))
cat(sprintf("AR    95%% CI [%.3f, %.3f]\n", ar[1], ar[2]))
import numpy as np
np.random.seed(1988)

# tF SE-multipliers: Lee, McCrary, Moreira & Porter (2022), Table 3 Panel A (5% level)
tF_F    = np.array([4.000, 5.002, 6.056, 7.085, 8.473, 9.835, 10.253, 13.048,
                    16.618, 20.721, 24.605, 33.457, 49.495, 68.930, 104.67])
tF_mult = np.array([9.519, 3.497, 2.602, 2.218, 1.935, 1.767, 1.727, 1.542,
                    1.407, 1.315, 1.256, 1.173, 1.096, 1.047, 1.000])

def tf_mult(F):
    if F < 3.84:    return np.inf      # no bounded interval below 3.84
    if F >= 104.67: return 1.0
    return float(np.interp(F, tF_F, tF_mult))

def make_data(n, pi, rho, beta=0.0):
    Z = np.random.standard_normal(n)
    v = np.random.standard_normal(n)
    e = np.random.standard_normal(n)
    u = rho * v + np.sqrt(1 - rho**2) * e   # corr(u, v) = rho -> endogeneity
    X = pi * Z + v
    return Z, X, beta * X + u

def iv_fit(Z, X, Y):                          # just-identified 2SLS / Wald
    Zc, Xc, Yc = Z - Z.mean(), X - X.mean(), Y - Y.mean()
    b = (Zc @ Yc) / (Zc @ Xc)
    r = Yc - b * Xc; n = len(Z)
    se = np.sqrt((r @ r) / (n - 1) * (Zc @ Zc)) / abs(Zc @ Xc)
    pi_hat = (Zc @ Xc) / (Zc @ Zc); fr = Xc - pi_hat * Zc
    F = (pi_hat / np.sqrt((fr @ fr) / (n - 2) / (Zc @ Zc))) ** 2
    return b, se, F

def ar_ci(Z, X, Y, grid):                     # invert the AR test, chi^2_1 cutoff
    Zc = Z - Z.mean(); n = len(Z); keep = []
    for b0 in grid:
        Wc = Y - b0 * X; Wc = Wc - Wc.mean()
        s = (Zc @ Wc) / (Zc @ Zc); res = Wc - s * Zc
        if (s / np.sqrt((res @ res) / (n - 2) / (Zc @ Zc))) ** 2 <= 3.841:
            keep.append(b0)
    return min(keep), max(keep)

n, pi, rho, reps = 200, 0.14, 0.95, 20000

# Part A: size of the naive 2SLS t-test when the null beta = 0 is TRUE
Fs, rej = [], 0
for _ in range(reps):
    Z, X, Y = make_data(n, pi, rho, 0.0)
    b, se, F = iv_fit(Z, X, Y); Fs.append(F)
    if abs(b / se) > 1.96: rej += 1
print(f"mean F={np.mean(Fs):.1f}  naive-t rejection @ nominal 5% = {rej/reps:.3f}")

# Part B: three intervals on one weak-instrument dataset
np.random.seed(1988)
Z, X, Y = make_data(n, pi, rho, 0.0)
b, se, F = iv_fit(Z, X, Y); m = tf_mult(F)
lo, hi = ar_ci(Z, X, Y, np.linspace(-5, 5, 100001))
print(f"beta_hat={b:.3f}  SE={se:.3f}  first-stage F={F:.2f}")
print(f"naive 95% CI [{b-1.96*se:.3f}, {b+1.96*se:.3f}]")
print(f"tF    95% CI [{b-1.96*m*se:.3f}, {b+1.96*m*se:.3f}]  (multiplier {m:.3f})")
print(f"AR    95% CI [{lo:.3f}, {hi:.3f}]")

The Python run reports a mean first-stage of about and a naive -test that rejects the true null 11% of the time — more than double its nominal 5%. The single dataset is more damning still. Its first-stage is , comfortably past the sacred , and the naive 95% interval is — it excludes the true value of zero and would be written up as a significant positive effect. The tF interval and the AR interval both contain the truth, as they should. (Exact figures differ slightly between R and Python because the random draws do; the pattern does not.) An instrument that passes the textbook screen still produces a confidently wrong conclusion under conventional inference.

Bottom Line

  • The “first-stage ” rule is broken. It was derived under homoskedasticity, conflates a bias criterion with a size criterion, and gives false comfort: a genuine 5% -test needs , not .
  • Gauge instrument strength with the effective -statistic (Montiel Olea–Pflueger), not the conventional or naive-robust first-stage .
  • For a robust default, report the Anderson–Rubin interval. It is valid for any instrument strength, costs nothing in the just-identified case when instruments are strong, and is honest enough to return an unbounded interval when the data genuinely cannot identify .
  • If you would rather keep the familiar -ratio, the tF procedure salvages it — inflate the standard error by the Table 3 factor at your observed — and, remarkably, yields shorter intervals than AR whenever both are bounded.
  • Either way, remember Young (2022): under clustered, non-iid errors a handful of high-leverage observations can drive both the point estimate and the first-stage . Check leverage and the sensitivity of your results to dropping a cluster or two before trusting any of these intervals.

Where to Learn More

For the canonical survey, Andrews, Stock, and Sun (2019, Annual Review of Economics) is the place to start — comprehensive, careful, and explicit about the heteroskedastic case. Keane and Neal (2024, Annual Review of Economics) is the most accessible statement of the “abandon the -test” position and worth reading for the power-asymmetry argument alone. Lee, McCrary, Moreira, and Porter (2022, American Economic Review) lay out the tF procedure with its adjustment tables, and their follow-up working paper extends it to the sharper VtF refinement. Young (2022, European Economic Review) is a bracing empirical reality check on how fragile published IV results actually are.

References

  • Anderson, T. W., & Rubin, H. (1949). Estimation of the parameters of a single equation in a complete system of stochastic equations. Annals of Mathematical Statistics, 20(1), 46–63.

  • Andrews, I., Stock, J. H., & Sun, L. (2019). Weak instruments in instrumental variables regression: Theory and practice. Annual Review of Economics, 11, 727–753.

  • Dufour, J.-M. (1997). Some impossibility theorems in econometrics with applications to structural and dynamic models. Econometrica, 65(6), 1365–1387.

  • Gleser, L. J., & Hwang, J. T. (1987). The nonexistence of confidence sets of finite expected diameter in errors-in-variables and related models. Annals of Statistics, 15(4), 1351–1362.

  • Keane, M. P., & Neal, T. (2024). A practical guide to weak instruments. Annual Review of Economics, 16, 185–212.

  • Lee, D. S., McCrary, J., Moreira, M. J., & Porter, J. (2022). Valid -ratio inference for IV. American Economic Review, 112(10), 3260–3290.

  • Lee, D. S., McCrary, J., Moreira, M. J., Porter, J., & Yap, L. (2023). What to do when you can’t use ‘1.96’ confidence intervals for IV. NBER Working Paper No. 31893.

  • Montiel Olea, J. L., & Pflueger, C. (2013). A robust test for weak instruments. Journal of Business & Economic Statistics, 31(3), 358–369.

  • Moreira, M. J. (2009). Tests with correct size when instruments can be arbitrarily weak. Journal of Econometrics, 152(2), 131–140.

  • Staiger, D., & Stock, J. H. (1997). Instrumental variables regression with weak instruments. Econometrica, 65(3), 557–586.

  • Stock, J. H., & Yogo, M. (2005). Testing for weak instruments in linear IV regression. In D. W. K. Andrews & J. H. Stock (Eds.), Identification and Inference for Econometric Models (pp. 80–108). Cambridge University Press.

  • Young, A. (2022). Consistency without inference: Instrumental variables in practical application. European Economic Review, 147, 104112.

item.title: Always-Valid \(p\)-Values and Online Multiple Testing
item.description:
13 min read

Background

Here is a confession. Early in my career, I would open the dashboard for a running A/B test every morning, and the moment the p-value dipped below I would call it: ship the variant, move on. It felt rigorous—I was, after all, looking at a real significance test. It was not rigorous. It was a near-perfect machine for manufacturing false positives, and I was the operator.

The problem is that the classical p-value is built for a world that modern data science has largely abandoned. That world has two defining features. First, you collect a fixed sample, compute one test statistic, and decide once. Second, when you do test many hypotheses, you have all of them—and all their p-values—sitting in front of you at the same time, so a procedure like Benjamini–Hochberg can sort them and pick a threshold. Neither feature survives contact with how experiments actually run today. A major tech company runs tens of thousands of A/B tests a year, each one monitored continuously as data trickle in. A genomics platform tests new gene knockouts as they are discovered, with no idea how many tests there will eventually be. A platform trial adds and drops treatment arms over months. Hypotheses arrive in a stream, and decisions have to be made now.

This breaks classical inference in two distinct places, and the single biggest source of confusion in this area is that people use the word “sequential” for both. I have written before about false discovery rate control and the many flavors of multiple testing adjustments; this post is about what happens to all of that machinery when time enters the picture. My goal is to draw a clean map of the terminology—sequential testing, always-valid p-values, online multiple testing—and then walk through the algorithms that make the streaming world tractable, following the exposition of Robertson, Wason and Ramdas (2023).

Notation

Hypotheses arrive at times . At each time we observe a p-value for a null hypothesis and must decide whether to reject before moving on to . We assume each is a valid p-value, meaning that whenever is true,

A testing procedure supplies a sequence of test levels and rejects via the rule Crucially, may depend only on the past decisions —not on the future, and not on the total number of tests, which may be unknown or infinite.

Let be the number of rejections (discoveries) by time , and let be the number of those that are false. Write .

For the inner process—repeated monitoring within a single experiment—a richer object is needed. An anytime-valid (or always-valid) p-value is a sequence indexed by the within-experiment sample size , such that The phrase “any stopping time” is the whole point: the guarantee holds no matter when—or why—you decide to stop looking.

A Closer Look

Three things people call “sequential”

Untangling the vocabulary is half the battle, so let me be explicit about three ideas that are routinely conflated.

Classical sequential testing concerns a single hypothesis whose data accumulate over time, with the sample size not fixed in advance. Wald’s sequential probability ratio test is the canonical example: keep sampling until the evidence is decisive, then stop. The hard part is that naive optional stopping destroys the type I error guarantee—if you re-test after every new observation and stop the first time you see , you will eventually cross that line even when the null is true.

Always-valid p-values are the modern device that fixes optional stopping. They are the inner process: a single experiment, monitored continuously, with a validity guarantee that survives stopping at any time for any reason.

Online multiple testing is a different problem entirely. Here a stream of distinct hypotheses arrives over time, each tested once, and the goal is to control a false discovery criterion across the whole stream while deciding each case in real time. This is the outer process.

The clean way to hold these together, due to Robertson, Wason and Ramdas, is the inner/outer framing. Each experiment in the stream is itself an inner sequential process—possibly monitored continuously—that ultimately emits a single valid p-value . The outer online-testing procedure then strings those together and controls error rates across experiments. The two layers compose cleanly: you can run an always-valid test inside each experiment and an online-FDR algorithm across them, and the guarantees stack. (A fourth cousin, the multi-armed bandit, also allocates effort across arms over time, but it is a single adaptive experiment, not a stream of hypotheses—so it sits outside this taxonomy.)

Always-valid p-values: the inner process

Why does peeking break a fixed- p-value? Because validity is a statement about a single pre-committed test. The event “ for some along the way” is a union over many looks, and its probability under the null can climb toward as you keep looking. A fixed-sample p-value makes no promise about that union.

An always-valid p-value is constructed precisely so that the union is controlled: simultaneously over all stopping times . The modern route to such objects runs through martingales and e-values—nonnegative statistics with expectation at most under the null, whose running products form a test supermartingale. Ville’s inequality then bounds the probability that the process ever exceeds a threshold, which is exactly the optional-stopping guarantee. The confidence-interval analog is the confidence sequence: a sequence of intervals that covers the true parameter simultaneously at all sample sizes with probability .

The practical payoff is enormous and is why this matters for industry. If an A/B testing platform reports always-valid p-values or confidence sequences, the experimenter can watch the dashboard continuously, stop whenever the result is decisive (or whenever business pressure demands), and still retain a valid inference. My morning-dashboard habit becomes legitimate—provided the platform does the math correctly. This is the rigorous foundation under continuous monitoring, and it complements the Bayesian approach to A/B tests that achieves a related flexibility from a different direction.

The catch is conservatism. Buying validity at every stopping time means always-valid p-values are, by construction, conservative: under the null they are stochastically larger than uniform. Hold onto that fact—it resurfaces below as an opportunity rather than a cost.

Error rates for the outer process

Now zoom out to the stream. Which error rate should an online procedure control? The false discovery proportion up to time is and the headline quantity, the false discovery rate, is its expectation, . The FDR is the right default for most applied work: it has a long track record in genetics, an intuitive reading as the expected share of discoveries that are wrong, and it scales gracefully to large streams.

Two variants show up constantly. The marginal FDR, , replaces the expectation of a ratio with a ratio of expectations. It is not identical to the FDR, but it is far more tractable, and many online algorithms can only be proven to control the mFDR. Treat it as a pragmatic stand-in when an FDR proof is unavailable for your setting. The false discovery exceedance goes the other way and controls a tail: . This is the right tool when the FDP can swing far from its mean—few hypotheses, or heavy dependence—and you want a guarantee about the realized proportion, not just its average. Finally, the familywise error rate, , the probability of any false rejection, remains the standard in confirmatory clinical trials where regulators demand it.

The defining constraint of the online setting is informational: when deciding on , you know only the past rejections, not the future and not even the eventual number of tests. The crudest response is alpha-spending—a Bonferroni-type split with . It controls the FWER, and hence the FDR, but at a ruinous price: the levels must shrink toward zero, so the power to reject collapses as grows. A procedure that becomes blind to discoveries simply because they arrive late is not viable. This is what motivates everything that follows.

Alpha-investing: testing on a budget

The breakthrough idea, originating with Foster and Stine (2008) and generalized by Aharoni and Rosset (2014), reframes testing as the management of an error budget called alpha-wealth. You start with wealth . Testing a hypothesis costs you some wealth —an investment. Making a discovery pays you back a reward . The wealth evolves as and must stay nonnegative, which constrains how aggressively you can test ().

The intuition for why a reward on rejection is legitimate—rather than a cheat—is worth internalizing. Look back at . Each genuine rejection increases the denominator, which means the procedure can now afford a few more false rejections in the numerator without breaching the target FDR. Discoveries literally buy you room to keep testing. As long as rejections keep coming, the wealth need not decay to zero, and the procedure can run indefinitely without the power collapse that sinks alpha-spending. This family is known as generalized alpha-investing (GAI), and a refined version, GAI++ (Ramdas et al., 2017), caps the per-rejection payout precisely enough to guarantee FDR control—a strict improvement over the original rules, which only controlled the mFDR.

LORD, SAFFRON, ADDIS

The wealth metaphor is intuitive but it was a “statistical” reframing by Ramdas et al. (2017) that produced the algorithms practitioners actually use. The idea is to maintain a running overestimate of the FDP and spend wealth to keep that estimate below —exactly the logic that powers Benjamini–Hochberg offline.

LORD (Javanmard and Montanari, 2018), and its uniform improvement LORD++, set each test level from a fixed, non-increasing sequence that sums to one, allocating a slice of the initial wealth plus a slice of the reward earned at each past rejection : Daunting at a glance, but readable: the first term spends a fraction of the starting budget, and every prior rejection injects fresh budget that is then doled out over future tests on the same schedule . LORD++ never spends more than it has earned, which is exactly why it keeps the FDP estimate below . It is best understood as the online analog of Benjamini–Hochberg, and under independence (more precisely, when the null p-values are conditionally super-uniform) it controls the FDR; Chen and Arias-Castro (2021) further show it is asymptotically as powerful as BH in a Gaussian model.

SAFFRON (Ramdas et al., 2018) makes LORD++ adaptive. It picks a threshold and refuses to spend wealth on “candidate” p-values larger than —since a large p-value was never going to be rejected anyway, why pay to test it? By estimating the proportion of true nulls and conserving wealth accordingly, SAFFRON delivers more power than LORD++ whenever a meaningful fraction of hypotheses are non-null with strong signals.

ADDIS (Tian and Ramdas, 2019) adds discarding on top of adaptivity. It explicitly throws away the most conservative nulls—the largest p-values—before testing. Here is where the earlier observation pays off: always-valid p-values are conservative under the null. A procedure that exploits conservative nulls is therefore a natural partner for an inner process built on continuous monitoring. The two layers of the inner/outer framing don’t just compose—they reinforce each other.

Where dependence bites

Which guarantee you actually get depends on how the p-values in the stream relate to one another, and the motivating examples span a clean spectrum. In A/B testing, separate experiments on different user populations yield roughly independent p-values—the friendly regime where full FDR control is provable. In platform trials, arms share a common control group, inducing known positive dependence among the p-values; the theory bends but does not break. In shared data repositories—public genomics databases queried by many researchers over time—the dependence is arbitrary and unknown, and only the most conservative guarantees survive. The practical lesson is to match the algorithm and the target error rate to the dependence structure you actually face, rather than reaching reflexively for FDR control that your setting cannot support.

Bottom Line

  • “Sequential” hides three distinct ideas. Classical sequential testing monitors one hypothesis with a growing sample; always-valid p-values make that monitoring rigorous under arbitrary stopping; online multiple testing controls error across a stream of distinct hypotheses. Keep them straight.
  • The inner/outer framing composes them: run an always-valid test inside each experiment, run an online-FDR algorithm across experiments, and the guarantees stack.
  • Always-valid p-values and confidence sequences are what make continuous monitoring of an A/B test legitimate. The price is conservatism under the null.
  • For the outer stream, prefer FDR as the default error rate, fall back to mFDR when an FDR proof is unavailable, and reach for FDX when the FDP can swing far from its mean. Avoid naive alpha-spending—its power decays to zero.
  • Among algorithms: LORD++ is the online BH and a safe default; SAFFRON adds adaptivity for power when signals are strong; ADDIS adds discarding, which pairs especially well with the conservative p-values that continuous monitoring produces.
  • Let dependence drive the choice: independence (A/B tests) permits full FDR control; positive dependence (platform trials) and arbitrary dependence (data repositories) demand more conservative guarantees.

Where to Learn More

The natural entry point is Robertson, Wason and Ramdas (2023), a Statistical Science review that surveys this entire literature with applied examples and simulations; this post follows its first two sections. For the foundations of the wealth metaphor, Foster and Stine (2008) introduced alpha-investing and Ramdas et al. (2017) put it on rigorous statistical footing with GAI++. Javanmard and Montanari (2018) developed LORD, with SAFFRON and ADDIS following in Ramdas et al. (2018) and Tian and Ramdas (2019). On the inner process, Howard et al. (2021) is the modern reference for confidence sequences, and Johari et al. (2021) is the canonical treatment of always-valid inference for continuous A/B-test monitoring.

References

Aharoni, E., and Rosset, S. (2014). Generalized -investing: definitions, optimality results and application to public databases. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B, 76(4), 771–794.

Chen, S., and Arias-Castro, E. (2021). On the power of some sequential multiple testing procedures. Annals of the Institute of Statistical Mathematics, 73(2), 311–336.

Foster, D. P., and Stine, R. A. (2008). -investing: a procedure for sequential control of expected false discoveries. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B, 70(2), 429–444.

Howard, S. R., Ramdas, A., McAuliffe, J., and Sekhon, J. (2021). Time-uniform, nonparametric, nonasymptotic confidence sequences. Annals of Statistics, 49(2), 1055–1080.

Javanmard, A., and Montanari, A. (2018). Online rules for control of false discovery rate and false discovery exceedance. Annals of Statistics, 46(2), 526–554.

Johari, R., Koomen, P., Pekelis, L., and Walsh, D. (2021). Always valid inference: continuous monitoring of A/B tests. Operations Research, 70(3), 1806–1821.

Ramdas, A., Yang, F., Wainwright, M. J., and Jordan, M. I. (2017). Online control of the false discovery rate with decaying memory. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 30.

Ramdas, A., Zrnic, T., Wainwright, M., and Jordan, M. (2018). SAFFRON: an adaptive algorithm for online control of the false discovery rate. Proceedings of the 35th International Conference on Machine Learning, 4286–4294.

Robertson, D. S., Wason, J. M. S., and Ramdas, A. (2023). Online multiple hypothesis testing. Statistical Science, 38(4), 557–575.

Tian, J., and Ramdas, A. (2019). ADDIS: an adaptive discarding algorithm for online FDR control with conservative nulls. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 32.

item.title: Double Descent Without the Neural Networks
item.description:
9 min read

Background

Every statistician learns a reflex early: a model that fits the training data perfectly — zero residuals, zero training error — has almost certainly overfit, and will predict badly out of sample. The bias–variance trade-off draws a tidy U: too simple and you are biased, too flexible and variance explodes, so you aim for the sweet spot in between. Interpolation is the cardinal sin.

Modern machine learning violates this reflex daily and gets away with it. Enormous neural networks that interpolate their training data — driving training error to exactly zero — routinely generalize beautifully. The phenomenon was dramatized under the name double descent by Belkin et al. (2019): push model complexity past the interpolation point and out-of-sample error, after spiking, comes back down again, sometimes below the classical sweet spot. The bias–variance U is only the first half of the story.

It is tempting to file this under “deep learning is weird.” I think that is exactly the wrong lesson, and the cleanest correction comes from Hastie, Montanari, Rosset, and Tibshirani (2022), who show that the entire phenomenon appears in the most classical model we have — linear least squares. No networks, no kernels, no mystery. My goal here is to read double descent the way a statistician should: as a story about interpolation, the geometry of the regime, and — above all — about what happens when you forget to regularize.

Notation

Take the usual linear model with i.i.d. data,

with and a design matrix that is . The single quantity that organizes everything is the overparametrization ratio

When the problem is underparametrized (more rows than columns, ordinary least squares applies); when it is overparametrized and least squares no longer has a unique solution. In that regime the natural estimator is the minimum-norm, or ridgeless, least squares solution

the least-squares fit of smallest Euclidean norm, and equivalently the limit of ridge regression as the penalty vanishes, . When it interpolates: for every training point. Write the signal-to-noise ratio as , and measure performance by the out-of-sample prediction risk at a fresh test point, , where .

A Closer Look

The curve

Here is the whole phenomenon in one curve. Fix , let sweep from small to large, fit ridgeless least squares at each step, and trace the out-of-sample risk against — in the isotropic case , holding the signal norm fixed so the comparison across is fair. The curve climbs as from below, blows up at the interpolation boundary, then descends a second time for before flattening out. In the isotropic limit Hastie et al. (2022) make this exact: as with ,

Below the boundary the risk is pure variance — no bias at all — and it diverges as . Above it, the risk is the sum of a bias term that grows with and a variance term that shrinks with . That tension is the engine of the second descent.

Bias up, variance down

Why does the risk fall again past ? The two components move in opposite directions.

The bias rises with for a reason that should be familiar if you have read about the regime: the minimum-norm solution is confined to the row space of , an -dimensional subspace sitting inside . As grows, that row space accounts for an ever-smaller slice of the ambient coefficient space, so the part of the data can never see — and which the min-norm rule simply zeroes out — grows with it. More parameters, more of left on the table, more bias.

The variance falls with , which feels backwards: surely more parameters means more variance? Not for the minimum-norm interpolator, and the intuition is in the name. Adding columns to gives the solver more directions over which to spread the fit, so the minimum-norm solution to generally has smaller norm as grows. A lower-norm interpolator is smoother and more stable — effectively more regularized. The minimum-norm criterion is doing the regularizing implicitly, for free.

The spike at is where these forces are both at their worst. With , the model has just barely enough capacity to interpolate and no slack to do it gracefully: is nearly singular, the fit threads every noisy point through a single contortion, and the variance term explodes. The interpolation threshold is not a sweet spot — it is the worst possible place to be.

It’s mostly a regularization story

Here is where I will be opinionated. The dramatic part of the double-descent curve — the divergence at — is an artifact of insisting on interpolation. And interpolation is a choice, not a law. Ridgeless least squares is just ridge regression with the penalty turned all the way down to zero, which is precisely the setting a statistician would never choose on purpose.

Hastie et al. (2022) make the point rigorously: optimally-tuned ridge regression dominates minimum-norm least squares at every value of and every SNR, in both well-specified and misspecified settings. The optimal ridge risk has no spike — tuning properly smooths the entire curve into a single well-behaved descent, and for misspecified models its global minimum sits right around , exactly where ridgeless regression is at its worst. The good news for practice is that this optimal penalty is estimable: leave-one-out cross-validation recovers it asymptotically.

So my reading is this. Double descent is real and the mathematics is beautiful, but the headline — more parameters fix overfitting — gets the moral backwards. The second descent shows that the minimum-norm interpolator carries a hidden, increasingly strong dose of regularization as grows. A statistician need not climb out to to enjoy it; she can dial it in directly with a ridge penalty and a cross-validation loop, and never visit the peak at all. The phenomenon is less “interpolation is secretly fine” than “under-regularization is bad, and overparametrization happens to re-regularize you on the way out.”

When overparametrization genuinely helps

That said, the strong version of the claim — that the global minimum of the risk can live deep in the overparametrized regime — is true, but only under structure, and it is worth knowing which structure.

In the isotropic, well-specified case it essentially never pays: even when produces a local minimum for , the global minimum stays in the underparametrized regime, and the risk only ever approaches the null risk from below as . Overparametrization buys you nothing you could not get more cheaply.

Two ingredients change the verdict. The first is misspecification: if the true regression function has components your features cannot represent, then adding features genuinely improves the approximation, and with enough signal the global minimum can move past the interpolation boundary. The second is structure in the covariance: when aligns with the leading eigenvectors of — the “latent space” model, where each new feature carries fresh information about a low-dimensional set of latent drivers — the risk can decrease monotonically across the whole overparametrized regime and reach its minimum as . This is the case that genuinely mirrors what people see in large networks, and it is no accident: the linear model with a learned, structured feature map is exactly the lazy-training (“neural tangent kernel”) linearization of an overparametrized network. The linear story is not an analogy — it is the same story.

Bottom Line

  • Double descent needs no neural networks. It appears in plain ridgeless least squares: out-of-sample risk follows the classical U for , diverges at the interpolation boundary , then descends a second time for .
  • The mechanics are pure bias–variance. Past the boundary, bias rises (min-norm is trapped in the -dimensional row space) while variance falls (the min-norm interpolant gets smoother as grows). The second descent is their tug-of-war.
  • The peak at is the worst place to be, not a sweet spot — is near-singular and variance explodes. It is an artifact of forcing interpolation.
  • It is largely a regularization story. Optimally-tuned ridge dominates ridgeless least squares everywhere and erases the spike; the implicit regularization of the min-norm rule is just doing, clumsily, what a ridge penalty does on purpose. Tune (e.g. by LOOCV) and the drama disappears.
  • Overparametrization wins globally only under structure: misspecification, high SNR, or aligned with the top eigenvectors of . Absent that, the classical sweet spot is still hard to beat.

Where to Learn More

The paper behind most of this post is Hastie, Montanari, Rosset, and Tibshirani (2022), which derives the exact asymptotic risk of ridgeless and ridge regression in the proportional regime and is the definitive statistical treatment of double descent. Belkin, Hsu, Ma, and Mandal (2019) is the paper that named the phenomenon and documented it across neural networks, random forests, and kernel machines — the empirical starting point. For the geometry of why the coefficients are unrecoverable but the predictions survive in the overparametrized regime, see my companion post on linear models when . And for the broader theory of when interpolation can be harmless, Bartlett, Long, Lugosi, and Tsybakov (2020) on “benign overfitting” is the natural next stop.

References

  • Bartlett, P. L., Long, P. M., Lugosi, G., & Tsybakov, A. B. (2020). Benign overfitting in linear regression. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 117(48), 30063–30070.

  • Belkin, M., Hsu, D., Ma, S., & Mandal, S. (2019). Reconciling modern machine-learning practice and the classical bias–variance trade-off. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 116(32), 15849–15854.

  • Hastie, T., Montanari, A., Rosset, S., & Tibshirani, R. J. (2022). Surprises in high-dimensional ridgeless least squares interpolation. The Annals of Statistics, 50(2), 949–986.

item.title: Linear Models and \(p > n\): What Can You Do?
item.description:
6 min read

Background

It seems intuitive that you cannot fit a regression with more predictors than observations. With parameters and only data points, the reasoning goes, you have more unknowns than equations, the system is underdetermined, and the whole enterprise collapses. Geneticists know this well.

This intuition is half right. It is true that when you cannot recover the coefficient vector — not even in principle, not with infinite sample or computing power. But it does not follow that the model is useless. The thing you usually care about — the prediction — can be perfectly well-defined even when itself is hopelessly ambiguous. Coefficients that are individually unknowable can combine into a quantity that is pinned down exactly. I find this one of the more beautiful facts in linear-model theory, and it sits at the heart of a recent expository paper by Ronald Christensen (2026).

I will spend most of the post on why is non-identifiable and what survives anyway, then briefly sketch the menu of estimators people actually reach for in this regime, and what “prediction” even means when half the parameter space is invisible to the data.

Notation

Start with the standard linear model

where

  • is an -vector of outcomes,
  • is a known design matrix,
  • is the -vector of unknown coefficients, and
  • is mean-zero noise.

The case of interest is , which forces the rank of to satisfy . Two subspaces do all the work.

  • The column space is the set of all fitted-value vectors the model can produce.
  • The row space is the span of the rows of — the directions in coefficient space that the data actually “see.”

Let denote the (orthogonal) projection onto the row space , so that any coefficient vector splits cleanly into

That decomposition is the whole story, so it is worth keeping in view.

A Closer Look

Why cannot be identified

Here is the crux. Every row of lives in by definition, which means annihilates anything orthogonal to the row space: . Multiply the model through and the consequence is immediate —

The component contributes nothing to , and therefore nothing to . The data are generated by alone. Whatever value takes — zero, enormous, anything — the distribution of is exactly the same. No estimator can distinguish between two coefficient vectors that agree on their row-space part and differ off it, because they produce statistically identical data.

When and has full column rank, the row space is all of , the projection is the identity, vanishes, and this issue never arises — which is why we rarely think about it. But the moment , the row space is a proper subspace of , the orthogonal complement is non-trivial, and a whole -dimensional flat of coefficient vectors fits the data equally well. That is non-identifiability, stated geometrically: is unknowable precisely along the directions the design never probes.

Non-unique coefficients, unique predictions

Now the payoff. The fitted-value vector is

and this object is unique even though is not. Geometrically, fitting projects onto the column space ; that projection is a single, well-defined point regardless of which of the infinitely many least-squares solutions you happened to compute. The same is true of the row-space part of the coefficients: is identifiable and estimable, while is not. So the situation is not “everything is lost,” but rather a clean partition — the row-space shadow of is recoverable, its orthogonal complement is forever hidden, and predictions live entirely in the recoverable part.

This is why high-dimensional regression is not the doomed exercise the equation-counting heuristic suggests. The coefficients are a fiction the data cannot fully resolve, but the model’s predictions are perfectly real. It is also a useful corrective to a habit many of us have — reading individual coefficients as if they meant something. With , an individual is whatever your estimator’s tie-breaking rule decided to make it; only its projection onto the row space carries information. (This tension between estimating coefficients and estimating predictions shows up even in well-posed problems; I have written about it in the difference between causal and predictive models.)

Four ways to pick a

If infinitely many coefficient vectors fit, every estimator is really a rule for choosing one — equivalently, a rule for deciding what to do off the row space, where the data are silent. Christensen lays out four that practitioners actually use, and the unifying thread is that they are all functions of the ordinary least-squares fit; they differ only in how they pin down the ambiguous directions.

  1. Minimum-norm least squares. Among all least-squares solutions, take the one with the smallest . This is exactly — the solution that sets the orthogonal component to zero and lives entirely in the row space . It is unique, it is what gradient descent from the origin converges to, and it is the natural reference point for the others.

  2. Penalized (regularized) least squares. Minimize for a penalty — ridge (), the LASSO (), elastic net, and so on. The penalty breaks the tie by preferring small or sparse coefficients, which generally makes the estimate unique.

  3. Spectral shrinkage. Working from the singular value decomposition of , shrink the components of the fit associated with each eigen-direction by a chosen factor. Ridge regression is the canonical special case (it shrinks the direction with eigenvalue by ), and as ridge converges to the minimum-norm solution.

  4. Principal component regression. Keep only the leading principal components of and regress on those, discarding the low-variance directions entirely. This is a hard-thresholding cousin of spectral shrinkage — zero weight below the cutoff, full weight above.

The differences among them are real but secondary to the shared structure: each one estimates the identifiable part from the data and then imposes some discipline on the unidentifiable part. For the theory behind when these choices recover the truth, see my post on the LASSO’s theoretical guarantees.

Bottom Line

  • With , the coefficient vector is not identifiable — the design only sees its projection onto the row space, and the orthogonal part is invisible to the data and to any prior.
  • Yet the fitted values and the row-space part are unique and estimable. Non-unique coefficients, unique predictions: that is the central fact, and it is what keeps high-dimensional regression alive.
  • Every estimator is a rule for choosing among the infinitely many fits, agreeing on and differing only off the row space.

References

  • Christensen, R. (2026). Linear Model Estimation and Prediction for . The American Statistician, 80(2), 232–240.

  • Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., & Friedman, J. (2009). The Elements of Statistical Learning (2nd ed.). New York: Springer.

item.title: A Simple Estimation Recipe for Targeted Learning
item.description:
6 min read

Background

Targeted learning is extremely popular in public health and epidemiology, yet it has never really made its way into the economics and econometrics world. It was introduced and extended by Mark van der Laan and coauthors in a long series of papers and books.The targeted learning class of methods enjoys very attractive theoretical properties. It is doubly robust and, under the right conditions, semiparametrically efficient, and it is method-agnostic in that it can plug in a wide range of machine learning algorithms.

This note aims to demystify targeted learning for a broader audience. From a practical standpoint, I find the best way to do that is through the lens of estimation, since that is where practitioners actually spend their time with a method. It is also the easiest way to compare it against the causal inference tools you already know — be it propensity score methods, double machine learning, or regression adjustment.

My focus is on the how-to and less on theoretical derivations or guarantees, and the overview borrows heavily from Schuler and Rose (2017). I deliberately stay with the basic version of targeted learning — targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) for the average treatment effect in a cross-sectional observational study. The framework extends to many other estimands, longitudinal data, and beyond, but those are out of scope here.

Notation

Suppose we observe i.i.d. draws of , where:

  • is a binary treatment (the “exposure”),
  • is a vector of confounders, and
  • is a continuous outcome.

Each unit has a pair of potential outcomes and , and the target is the average treatment effect (ATE),

Two nuisance functions do all the work. The outcome regression is the conditional mean of the outcome,

and the propensity score is the conditional probability of treatment,

For to carry a causal interpretation we need the usual trio of assumptions — no unmeasured confounding, positivity (), and SUTVA — which I will take as given since the focus here is estimation, not identification.

A concrete example to fix ideas, taken from the paper: is whether a person exercises regularly, is a depression score, and collects sex, use of psychosocial therapy, and antidepressant use.

A Closer Look

Two Nuisances, Three Estimators

The reason TMLE is easy to place on a mental map is that it shares its ingredients with methods you already use. G-computation (regression adjustment) models only the outcome regression and then averages the predicted difference. Inverse probability weighting (IPW) models only the propensity score and reweights the observed outcomes. Each leans entirely on getting one of the two nuisances right.

TMLE uses both. It starts from an outcome-regression prediction and then nudges it using the propensity score, in a way designed specifically for the ATE. That extra step is what buys double robustness: the final estimate is consistent if either or is correctly estimated, not necessarily both. If both are consistent, the estimator is also efficient. This is the practical payoff, and it is why the method is worth the extra bookkeeping.

The Recipe: Four Steps

Here is the whole procedure for the ATE, stripped to its essentials. One technical preliminary: because the targeting step runs through a logistic regression, the continuous outcome is first rescaled to the unit interval , and everything is undone at the end.

NoteAlgorithm: TMLE for the ATE
  1. Initial outcome model. Fit and use it to predict each unit’s pair of potential outcomes, and .
  2. Propensity model. Fit , giving and .
  3. Targeting step. Form the “clever covariate” , run a one-parameter logistic regression of on using the initial as an offset to estimate a fluctuation , and update the predicted potential outcomes on the logit scale.
  4. Plug-in estimate. Average the difference between the updated potential outcomes: .

The only genuinely new object is the clever covariate . Note that it looks a lot like an inverse-probability weight — and that is no accident — but here it enters as a regressor in the fluctuation rather than as a weight. Step 3 is the “targeting”: it tilts the initial fit just enough to remove first-order bias in the direction that matters for the ATE, while leaving the rest of the outcome model alone.

Why Bother With the Targeting Step?

A fair question is why we do not simply stop after step 1 and report the average difference in predicted outcomes — that is exactly G-computation. The answer is robustness. A plain outcome-model plug-in is only as good as that one model; if it is misspecified, the estimate is biased, full stop. The targeting step injects information from the propensity score so that a mistake in one of the two models can be absorbed by the other. In the paper’s simulation, when either the outcome or the exposure model is deliberately misspecified, TMLE stays essentially unbiased, while G-computation and IPW pick up large bias from the same misspecification.

Two practical notes follow from this. First, TMLE is a substitution (plug-in) estimator — the ATE is computed by plugging fitted values into the target functional — which makes it more stable than estimating-equation methods like IPW when propensity scores get close to or . Second, because the recipe never cares how and are estimated, you should estimate them with flexible machine learning rather than hand-specified regressions. In practice this means an ensemble (the super learner), which lets the data choose among a library of ML methods instead of betting on one functional form.

Bottom Line

  • Targeted maximum likelihood estimation is a very popular class of methods in the public health and epidemiology communities, and underused elsewhere.
  • It is doubly robust — consistent if either the outcome regression or the propensity score is right — and semiparametrically efficient when both are.
  • It is flexible: the recipe is agnostic to how the two nuisance functions are estimated, so it accommodates a wide range of machine learning algorithms (ideally an ensemble).
  • In essence, TMLE is outcome-regression plus propensity-score modeling, with one extra step that removes bias from the outcome model through a clever use of the estimated propensity score.

References

  • Schuler, M. S., & Rose, S. (2017). Targeted maximum likelihood estimation for causal inference in observational studies. American Journal of Epidemiology, 185(1), 65–73.

  • van der Laan, M. J., & Rose, S. (2011). Targeted Learning: Causal Inference for Observational and Experimental Data. Springer.

  • van der Laan, M. J., & Rubin, D. (2006). Targeted maximum likelihood learning. The International Journal of Biostatistics, 2(1), Article 11.

item.title: The Role of Instrumental Variables in Randomized Experiments
item.description:
6 min read

Background

Instrumental variables (IV) are econometricians’ pride. They underpin a great many scientific findings, and even the Nobel committee in Stockholm seems fond of them, judging by the laureates it picks. Yet their prominence outside academia is limited, and so I have not featured them on this blog as often as their importance warrants. Today I change that.

My goal here is to give an overview of how one can use instrumental variables inside randomized experiments. The material draws on the recent NEJM survey by Angrist, Gao, Hull, and Yeh (2025), which makes the case for IV methods in clinical trials. I keep the mathematical rigor to a minimum and shine the spotlight on the practical side of the ideas. A caveat worth stating up front: IVs are arguably even more powerful in the absence of randomization, where they rescue observational studies from confounding — but that is a separate topic for another day.

The setup that motivates everything below is simple and familiar to anyone who has run an experiment: randomized trials often fail to go according to plan. People assigned to treatment do not take it; people assigned to control find a way to get it anyway. IVs offer a principled way to recover causal effects when that happens.

Notation

Index participants by . Let

  • be the outcome.
  • denote random assignment to treatment, and
  • denote the treatment actually received.

In a clean experiment these coincide, but with noncompliance they need not: a participant can have (assigned but untreated) or (a control who crossed over). The instrument is the randomized assignment ; the endogenous variable is the received treatment .

Following the potential-outcomes framework, write and for participant ’s outcome with and without treatment; the individual causal effect is

forever unobservable since only one of the two is realized.

Likewise, let be the treatment would take under assignment . This last object lets us partition the population into four latent types:

  • Compliers take treatment when assigned and not otherwise: .
  • Always-takers take treatment regardless: .
  • Never-takers refuse regardless: .
  • Defiers do the opposite of their assignment — assumed away below.

Three quantities organize the analysis.

  1. The first stage is the effect of assignment () on treatment () received, i.e. the difference in treatment rates between the assigned-treatment and assigned-control groups — also called compliance.
  2. The reduced form (or intention-to-treat, ITT, effect) is the effect of assignment () on the outcome ().
  3. The local average treatment effect (LATE) is the average causal effect among compliers.

The whole framework rests on four assumptions:

  1. random assignment of ;
  2. a first-stage (relevance) condition — assignment must actually move treatment, so compliance is non-zero;
  3. monotonicity — assignment never pushes anyone away from treatment, which rules out defiers; and
  4. the exclusion restriction — assignment affects the outcome only through treatment receipt, never directly.

A Closer Look

Use Case #1: Noncompliance

In a trial where everyone complies, , and the difference in mean outcomes by assignment is the average treatment effect. Noncompliance breaks this.

Suppose treatment effects are constant and equal to , so that

Then a little algebra on the assignment-based comparison gives

The intuition is that the ITT effect gets diluted: when only a fraction of those assigned actually take treatment, the assignment-versus-assignment comparison mixes in untreated people on the treatment side and crossovers on the control side, shrinking the apparent effect toward zero. If compliance is, say, , the ITT understates the true effect by more than half.

The fix falls right out of the identity. Dividing the reduced form by the first stage recovers the effect:

This ratio of differences in means is the heart of IV analysis — the Wald estimator. When effects are heterogeneous, the LATE theorem (Imbens and Angrist, 1994) tells us this same ratio identifies not some universal but the average effect for compliers:

Use Case #2: Selection Bias

The second problem IVs solve is subtler, and it is where practitioners most often go wrong. Faced with noncompliance, the tempting move is a per-protocol or as-treated analysis: just compare people by the treatment they actually received, . The trouble is that is not randomized. The people who comply, cross over, or refuse differ systematically, so comparing by treatment received reintroduces exactly the confounding that randomization was designed to eliminate.

The IV approach sidesteps this entirely by comparing groups defined by randomized assignment , not by . The comparison is apples-to-apples — the complier subpopulation on the treated side is statistically identical to the complier subpopulation on the control side, differing only in the luck of the draw. The estimate is a per-protocol effect, but one purged of selection bias. As a bonus, although individual compliers cannot be identified, their average baseline characteristics can be computed, letting the analyst check how representative they are of the broader patient population.

Bottom Line

  • Instrumental variables are ubiquitous in academic econometrics and applied microeconomics; their footprint in industry remains comparatively small.
  • Randomized experiments do not always go according to plan — noncompliance and crossovers are common in pragmatic and clinical trials.
  • IVs come to the rescue for two distinct, thorny problems: noncompliance and selection bias.
  • The price is interpretational: IV identifies the LATE, an effect specific to the compliers induced by the instrument — not necessarily the average effect for the whole population. Whether that is the estimand you want is a question worth asking before you run the regression.

Where to Learn More

The two foundational papers are Imbens and Angrist (1994), which introduced the LATE theorem, and Angrist, Imbens, and Rubin (1996), which recast IV estimation in the potential-outcomes language used throughout this note. For a complete and famously readable treatment, Angrist and Pischke’s Mostly Harmless Econometrics (2009) remains the standard reference. The clinical-trials framing here follows Angrist, Gao, Hull, and Yeh (2025), an accessible survey aimed at medical researchers that I recommend to anyone who wants to see these ideas applied end-to-end on a real trial.

References

  • Angrist, J. D., Gao, C., Hull, P., & Yeh, R. W. (2025). Instrumental variables in randomized trials. NEJM Evidence, 4(4).

  • Angrist, J. D., Imbens, G. W., & Rubin, D. B. (1996). Identification of causal effects using instrumental variables. Journal of the American Statistical Association, 91(434), 444–455.

  • Angrist, J. D., & Pischke, J.-S. (2009). Mostly Harmless Econometrics: An Empiricist’s Companion. Princeton University Press.

  • Imbens, G. W., & Angrist, J. D. (1994). Identification and estimation of local average treatment effects. Econometrica, 62(2), 467–475.

item.title: Lasso and Its Theoretical Guarantees
item.description:
7 min read

Background

Every data scientist has heard of the Lasso, and I have written plenty about it here as well — its many flavors, its oracle property, and its use in estimating heterogeneous treatment effects, among other things. It is appealing for several reasons: simplicity, cheap and fast computation, interpretability, and a solid theoretical foundation.

In this note I focus on that last item — what makes the Lasso attractive from a theoretical standpoint. The goal is to give a high-level overview of the kinds of guarantees the Lasso promises. The literature here is heavy and daunting, so I keep the mathematical rigor to a minimum. I am after intuition: what can the Lasso deliver and under what conditions?

The punchline, which I will return to repeatedly, is that these guarantees are not free. Each one rests on assumptions about the design matrix, the noise, and the true signal — and those assumptions get stronger as the promises get bolder.

Notation

Consider the usual linear model

where is the outcome, is the design matrix, is the true coefficient vector, and is mean-zero noise with variance . The interesting regime is high-dimensional, where can be comparable to or much larger than .

The signal is assumed sparse: only a few coordinates of are non-zero. Let

denote the support and its size. Sparsity is what makes the problem tractable when .

The Lasso can be written in two equivalent ways. The penalized (Lagrangian) form is

and the constrained form is

For every there is a that yields the same solution. The penalty is what does the work: unlike the (ridge) penalty, it has corners that produce exact zeros, giving simultaneous shrinkage and selection. The tuning parameter controls the strength of regularization.

A Closer Look

I will examine three broad groups of theoretical results. Each comes with its own assumptions and works only under specific conditions — sparse true support, a particular growth rate for relative to , a well-behaved design matrix, and so on. I have ordered the sections in roughly ascending order of how demanding those assumptions are. This ordering is the most important takeaway of the post: prediction is cheap, estimation costs more, and exact support recovery is expensive.

Prediction Error

This is the most basic guarantee. It says the Lasso will predict “well enough” — the in-sample prediction error will not exceed a certain bound.

Formally, with the penalty chosen at the canonical rate , one can show that with high probability

This is the so-called slow rate.

What is remarkable is how little it requires: essentially no conditions on the design matrix beyond standardizing the columns. All you need is for to be large enough to dominate the noise — which is exactly what the scaling ensures. The term is the price of searching over candidate predictors, and it enters only logarithmically, which is why the Lasso tolerates enormous .

If you are willing to assume more about the design (see the next section), the bound sharpens to the fast rate,

which scales with the number of true signals rather than with .

Estimation Error

Here the question shifts from predictions to the coefficients themselves — how close is to ? This is a harder problem, and it requires a condition on the design matrix.

The standard assumption is the restricted eigenvalue (RE) condition. Loosely, it requires that behave like a well-conditioned matrix — bounded away from singularity — but only over the directions that matter, namely vectors that are approximately sparse. We cannot ask to be invertible outright, since makes that impossible; the restriction to a sparse cone is what rescues the argument.

Under the RE condition with constant , and again ,

The intuition is that strong correlations among predictors flatten the objective function in certain directions, making the coefficients poorly identified even when predictions are fine. The RE condition rules out the worst of this. When it holds, the error decays at essentially the rate an oracle who knew would achieve, up to the factor.

You know to never take the coefficients — or the implied -values — from a Lasso fit too seriously without an extreme degree of caution. The shrinkage that makes the Lasso work also biases every non-zero coefficient toward zero, and standard inference ignores the selection step entirely. I have written about hypothesis testing in linear ML models elsewhere on the blog; the estimation bounds above are about rates, not about valid confidence intervals.

Support Recovery

This is the most intriguing guarantee and, fittingly, the most expensive. It is relevant for model selection rather than prediction: it asks whether the Lasso picks out exactly the right set of variables, , where .

The goal is selection consistency,

Two strong conditions are typically needed. The first is the irrepresentable condition, which limits how strongly the irrelevant predictors () can be correlated with the relevant ones ():

for some . This is close to necessary, not merely sufficient — if it fails, the Lasso tends to bring spurious variables into the model. The second is a beta-min condition: the smallest non-zero coefficient must be detectably large, . Signals weaker than the noise floor simply cannot be distinguished from zero.

The practical implication is sobering. Exact support recovery is fragile: it demands that the truly important variables be only mildly correlated with the unimportant ones, and that no real signal be too small. Both are easy to violate with observational data. This is also why I treated this topic separately in my post on the oracle property — standard Lasso generally does not achieve oracle-style selection, and the fixes (adaptive Lasso, nonconvex penalties like SCAD and MCP) exist precisely to relax the irrepresentable condition.

Bottom Line

  • The Lasso is among the most popular machine learning methods, and a good part of its appeal comes from a strong theoretical foundation.
  • Under suitable conditions, one can show that the Lasso performs well across three distinct tasks — prediction, coefficient estimation, and exact support recovery..
  • Prediction error is nearly free; estimation error needs a restricted-eigenvalue-type condition on the design; support recovery additionally needs an irrepresentable condition and a minimum-signal-strength condition.
  • Not all of these guarantees will hold on any given dataset. The researcher must evaluate the regularity conditions carefully for each claim she makes.

Where to Learn More

For a focused, modern treatment, “Statistical Learning with Sparsity” by Hastie, Tibshirani, and Wainwright (2015) is the natural starting point, with “The Elements of Statistical Learning” (2009) as gentler background. For the theory nerds, Bühlmann and van de Geer’s “Statistics for High-Dimensional Data” (2011) and Wainwright’s “High-Dimensional Statistics” (2019) are the standard references, though both are too dense for most mortals.

References

  • Belloni, A., Chernozhukov, V., & Wang, L. (2011). Square-root lasso: Pivotal recovery of sparse signals via conic programming. Biometrika, 98(4), 791–806.

  • Bickel, P. J., Ritov, Y., & Tsybakov, A. B. (2009). Simultaneous analysis of Lasso and Dantzig selector. The Annals of Statistics, 37(4), 1705–1732.

  • Bühlmann, P., & van de Geer, S. (2011). Statistics for High-Dimensional Data: Methods, Theory and Applications. Springer.

  • Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., & Friedman, J. (2009). The Elements of Statistical Learning: Data Mining, Inference, and Prediction (2nd ed.). Springer.

  • Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., & Wainwright, M. (2015). Statistical Learning with Sparsity: The Lasso and Generalizations. CRC Press.

  • Raskutti, G., Wainwright, M. J., & Yu, B. (2011). Minimax rates of estimation for high-dimensional linear regression over -balls. IEEE Transactions on Information Theory, 57(10), 6976–6994.

  • Tibshirani, R. (1996). Regression shrinkage and selection via the lasso. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B (Methodological), 58(1), 267–288.

  • van de Geer, S., Bühlmann, P., Ritov, Y., & Dezeure, R. (2014). On asymptotically optimal confidence regions and tests for high-dimensional models. The Annals of Statistics, 42(3), 1166–1202.

  • Wainwright, M. J. (2009). Sharp thresholds for high-dimensional and noisy sparsity recovery using -constrained quadratic programming (Lasso). IEEE Transactions on Information Theory, 55(5), 2183–2202.

  • Wainwright, M. J. (2019). High-Dimensional Statistics: A Non-Asymptotic Viewpoint. Cambridge University Press.

  • Zhao, P., & Yu, B. (2006). On model selection consistency of Lasso. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 7, 2541–2563.

item.title: Second-Generation \(p\)-Values: A Worthy Idea That Did Not Catch On
item.description:
7 min read

Background

Hardly a data science interview goes by without the person in power bringing up the definition of the infamous -value. Normally, chaos ensues. For good or ill, -values are omnipresent in a data scientist’s world. A grad school professor of mine once described them as summaries of an entire dataset into a single number — or even, I would add, into a single yes/no decision.

The downsides of -values have been well understood and widely publicized for decades. They conflate effect size and precision, they are routinely misinterpreted, and they say nothing about scientific relevance or practical importance. Famously, the American Statistical Association issued a formal statement on their use and misuse (Wasserstein and Lazar, 2016). Predictably, there has been no shortage of proposed alternatives — -values, Bayes factors, posterior probabilities, and more.

In this note I describe one such alternative: the second-generation -value (SGPV) of Blume et al. (2019). The point is to broaden how we think about using data to test scientific claims, and to get acquainted with a genuinely different approach. I keep the mathematical rigor to a minimum and focus on the intellectual idea behind the proposal.

Notation

Suppose we are interested in some parameter — a difference in means, a log odds ratio, a regression coefficient, whatever the scientific question demands. Classical testing pits a simple (point) null hypothesis against everything else:

A simple hypothesis pins to a single value; a composite hypothesis allows a whole set of values. The key move in what follows is to replace the point null with a composite one.

Let denote an interval estimate of — for concreteness, a 95% confidence interval — with length .

Also, let

denote an interval null hypothesis: the range of effect sizes that are too small to matter scientifically, with length . This interval includes the exact null but widens it to absorb effects that are technically non-zero yet practically trivial. Choosing its width is a scientific judgment, made before seeing the data.

A Closer Look

The First-Generation P-Value

Start with the object everyone already knows. We observe a dataset, and the -value is the probability of seeing data at least as extreme — weirder, loosely speaking — than what we actually observed, if the null hypothesis were true.

A large -value means the data are unsurprising under the null and therefore offer no evidence against it. Famously, this is not the probability that the null is true; getting at that requires a Bayesian apparatus and a prior. This single number has carried an enormous load for nearly a century, and that is precisely the source of trouble.

The Problem

The classical -value conflates two things a practitioner usually wants to keep separate: the size of an effect and the precision with which it is estimated. A tiny, scientifically meaningless effect will produce an arbitrarily small -value once the sample is large enough, because precision alone drives significance. In the era of big data this is not a hypothetical — it is the default failure mode.

The deeper issue is the point null itself. The hypothesis exactly is almost never true and rarely even interesting. Two treatments are essentially never identical to infinite precision; the relevant question is whether they differ by an amount that matters. Statistical significance, as classically defined, simply does not speak to that question.

The Main Idea

The SGPV, which I will denote , replaces the point null with the interval null and then measures how much the interval estimate overlaps it. It is a proportion, not a tail-area probability:

where is the length of the overlap between the two intervals. When the interval estimate is reasonably precise (specifically, ), the correction term is and the formula reduces to the clean fraction — the share of the interval estimate that falls inside the null zone. The correction factor only kicks in when is very wide relative to the null, capping the statistic to reflect that the data are simply too imprecise to say much.

The interpretation is direct:

  • : the interval estimate does not overlap the null at all. The data are incompatible with a trivial effect and support a scientifically meaningful one.
  • : the interval estimate lies entirely inside the null. The data support only the null premise — something a classical -value can never tell you.
  • : the data are inconclusive, with being the maximally inconclusive case.

Quick Example
